title: Repairing a loose chair leg with a corner glue block
subtitle: Reinforce a rocking or loose chair leg joint by gluing and screwing a timber corner block into the seat frame
excerpt: How to repair a loose chair leg by fitting a timber corner glue block inside the seat frame angle. Covers fitting a new block to reinforce a joint that has loosened without needing to take the chair apart or inject adhesive.
sourceNotes: Practical Furniture Restoration, Julian Worthington (2004). Newey and Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery (1905, public domain). City and Guilds Level 2 Furniture Restoration.

glossaryTerms.definition: A triangular or square block of hardwood glued and screwed into the inside corner of a chair seat frame at each leg-to-rail junction. The block spans the angle between two rails and the leg stile, dramatically stiffening the joint by distributing the racking force across a much larger glue area than the mortise-and-tenon alone. Original factory corner blocks are often visible on the underside of old chairs; replacement blocks use the same principle.

content.text: A chair leg that rocks or creaks has a loose mortise-and-tenon (or dowel) joint between the leg and the seat rail. The cleanest repair requires taking the chair apart, cleaning the old glue, and re-gluing; but this is not always practical on a chair with multiple failed joints. A

is the practical alternative: a triangular timber block glued and screwed into the seat frame corner, stiffening the joint without disassembly. It is not as strong as a properly re-glued joint, but it is durable enough for normal use and takes 30 minutes per corner.

Triangular corner blocks cut from 50 x 50 mm hardwood (beech or oak); cut at 45 degrees across the diagonal to give a 50 mm face on two sides. PVA woodworking glue. 35 mm screws (two per block; one into each rail). A cordless drill with a countersink bit. G-cramps to hold the block while the glue sets. Sandpaper for cleaning surfaces.

Turn the chair upside down on a padded surface. Examine the inside of the seat frame at the loose leg. Remove any old corner block if one is present; lever off with the utility knife. Clean off old glue residue from the corner surfaces with 80-grit sandpaper. The surfaces must be clean and free of old glue for the new block to bond effectively.

Cut the corner block so its two flat faces sit flat against the two rails meeting at the corner; the hypotenuse face is open toward the seat centre. The block faces should contact the full rail width if possible. Drill and countersink two pilot holes through the block: one hole into each rail, angled slightly toward the frame. Check the fit by dry-assembling: the block should sit fully in contact with both rails without a gap.

Apply PVA to both faces of the corner block. Press the block firmly into the corner. Drive the two pilot-hole screws to pull the block tight against both rails. If cramps will reach, apply a G-cramp across each pair of faces while the glue sets. Allow 2 hours minimum before testing. Do not load the repaired chair until the glue has dried for 24 hours.
Injecting adhesive into a loose mortise (supplementary method)

If the mortise-and-tenon joint has only minor play, inject PVA into the gap with a glue syringe before fitting the corner block. Work the adhesive into the joint gap with the tip of the syringe while moving the leg back and forth slightly to distribute the glue. Wipe off the excess. Apply the corner block while the injected adhesive is still wet, so both the injection and the block set together.

attrs.symptom: Chair still rocks after fitting the corner block
attrs.cause: The leg-to-rail joint has more play than the corner block alone can correct, or the opposite corner joint is also loose.
attrs.fix: Fit a corner block to all four corners. Check whether the leg joint itself needs the mortise-and-tenon re-glued by attempting to move the leg independently of the frame.
